chunk_summary: Stepwise GERD management in children with neurodisability emphasizes time-limited PPI trials, non-pharmacologic measures, and careful evaluation of alarm features to avoid over-treatment and address multifactorial symptoms.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise approach to GERD management in children with neurodisability.
Learn the criteria for initiating and deprescribing PPIs in pediatric GERD cases.
Recognize alarm features requiring referral or further evaluation in GERD management.
Evaluate the role of non-pharmacologic measures such as feeding adjustments and positioning.
key_facts: PPI trials should be time-limited to 4–8 weeks with planned review and deprescribing strategies.
Administer PPIs 30–60 minutes before meals for optimal efficacy.
Prokinetics like domperidone or metoclopramide are not recommended due to uncertain efficacy and adverse effects.
Non-pharmacologic measures include avoiding overfeeding, adjusting feeding volumes and frequency, and optimizing positioning during and after feeds.
Alarm features requiring referral include persistent dysphagia, weight loss, recurrent hematemesis, and severe feeding aversion.
reference_quote: NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN guidelines recommend time-limited PPI trials (4–8 weeks) with planned review and deprescribing strategies to avoid indefinite escalation of therapy.
document_key_topics: Referral criteria for alarm features in GERD
